eras.name: Sukhothai & Ayutthaya Kingdoms
eras.description: The great Thai kingdoms developed a sophisticated textile culture centered on silk weaving and the distinctive wrapped garment traditions. Court dress was strictly codified by royal decree, with specific colors, fabrics, and ornaments designating rank and occasion.
eras.aiImagePrompt: An Ayutthaya-era Thai court lady wearing a gold-threaded silk pha nung with a draped sabai across one shoulder, ornate gold tiara and arm cuffs, standing in a teak palace with lotus ponds, warm candlelight.

eras.name: Rattanakosin & Royal Silk Era
eras.description: The Bangkok dynasty refined Thai court dress into its most elaborate forms. King Chulalongkorn modernized national dress by blending Western tailoring with Thai silk traditions, while regional weaving centers produced silk of extraordinary quality.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Siamese nobleman in the court of King Chulalongkorn wearing a white high-collared jacket over a gold brocade chong kraben, jeweled belt and royal decorations, Grand Palace architecture in the background.

eras.name: Jim Thompson Silk Revival
eras.description: American architect Jim Thompson single-handedly revived the dying Thai silk industry, transforming village weaving into an international luxury brand. His efforts brought Thai silk to Hollywood and haute couture, making it synonymous with Southeast Asian luxury.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A 1960s fashion editorial in Jim Thompson's Bangkok teak house, featuring a Western model in a jewel-toned Thai silk sheath dress, surrounded by antique Southeast Asian art, lush tropical garden visible through wooden shutters.

eras.name: Queen Sirikit National Dress
eras.description: Queen Sirikit commissioned designer Pierre Balmain to codify eight official Thai national dress forms in the 1960s. Her patronage elevated Thai fashion diplomacy and ensured traditional garments remained prestigious in the modern era.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Thai woman in the Chakkri formal dress, a floor-length Thai silk gown in royal blue with gold brocade sash draped over one shoulder, tiered diamond jewelry, posing in a gilded throne hall.

eras.name: Bangkok Streetwear & Club Culture
eras.description: Bangkok emerged as Southeast Asia's streetwear capital, driven by Chatuchak Market's indie fashion scene and a booming nightlife culture. Thai designers blended hip-hop influences with Buddhist iconography and tropical aesthetics.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Bangkok street style scene at Chatuchak weekend market, a young Thai creative in oversized graphic tee with Thai script, wide-leg cargo pants, chunky sneakers, neon market signs in the background, night atmosphere.

eras.name: Thai Fashion Goes Global
eras.description: Thai designers like Sretsis, Asava, and Pipatchara have gained international followings. Bangkok Fashion Week has attracted global buyers, while Thai beauty and fashion content creators dominate Southeast Asian social media.
eras.aiImagePrompt: A Bangkok Fashion Week runway moment with a model in a structured tropical-print pantsuit with Thai silk lapels, modern concrete venue with dramatic projection mapping, editorial lighting.

textiles.name: Mudmee (Mat Mii) Silk
textiles.description: An ancient ikat technique producing Thai silk with geometric patterns created by resist-dyeing the threads before weaving, traditionally in deep jewel tones.
textiles.technique: Warp ikat resist-dyeing with natural dyes, woven on frame looms with continuous weft

textiles.name: Pha Yok Thong
textiles.description: A luxurious gold-brocade silk reserved for royal and ceremonial garments, featuring raised supplementary weft patterns of mythical creatures and floral motifs.
textiles.technique: Supplementary gold and silver metallic weft weaving on a drawloom

textiles.name: Thai Silk (Jim Thompson)
textiles.description: Internationally renowned handwoven silk characterized by its natural luster, slightly textured surface, and vivid colors achieved through a unique double-shuttle technique.
textiles.technique: Handloom weaving with two shuttles creating different colored warp and weft for iridescent effect

textiles.name: Pha Khao Ma
textiles.description: A versatile checkered or plaid cotton cloth used as a loincloth, towel, head wrap, and all-purpose garment by Thai men across all social classes.
textiles.technique: Plain weave cotton with check patterns in indigo, red, and natural white

textiles.name: Yok Dok Brocade
textiles.description: An intricate supplementary weft technique creating raised floral and geometric patterns, used for ceremonial sinh skirts and formal garments.
textiles.technique: Continuous supplementary weft brocading on a traditional Northern Thai loom

climate.fashionImplication: Intense heat and humidity drive preference for lightweight, breathable silks and cottons with loose draping. The monsoon season requires practical layering, while air-conditioned urban interiors create demand for light jackets and wraps.

culturalInfluences: Theravada Buddhism and monastic saffron robes
Royal court ceremony and dress codification
Japanese and Korean pop culture (J-pop, K-pop influence)
Southeast Asian night market and street culture

emergingTrends: Sustainable fashion using lotus and pineapple fiber
Tropical tailoring for international resort markets
notableEvents: Support Arts & Crafts International Centre of Thailand (SACICT) Craft exhibitions
Queen Sirikit Museum of Textiles exhibitions
